record_office_terminology.land_records: Town Clerk (New England pattern — land records at town level, not county)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Probate Court (one of 14 probate districts, one per county)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Town clerks record marriages. State registration began 1857; town records often earlier.
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1857 (state registration). Town clerks recorded births, marriages, and deaths from settlement, often decades earlier. FamilySearch has microfilmed many town vital record volumes.

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Vermont Record Losses; Vermont State Archives guides. Vermont has no burned counties — record survival is generally excellent across all 14 counties.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Vermont has no burned counties. Record survival is excellent statewide, benefiting from the New England town clerk tradition. Vermont's compact size and stable county boundaries since 1835 contribute to good preservation.

administrative_notes: Vermont declared independence as the Vermont Republic on January 15, 1777, and operated as an independent republic until admitted as the 14th state on March 4, 1791. Vermont was the first state to ban adult slavery in its constitution (1777). Originally organized with Bennington and Cumberland counties (Cumberland was ceded to New York). By 1835, all 14 present-day counties were established. Vermont uses a New England town government system — vital records and land records are maintained by town clerks, not county officials. Probate is at the county level. The Vermont State Archives in Middlesex (near Montpelier) is the central state repository. The Vermont Historical Society in Barre holds the premier genealogical collection. Key jurisdictional note: Pre-1777, the territory was claimed by both New Hampshire (which granted town charters as the 'New Hampshire Grants') and New York (which organized Cumberland and Gloucester counties). Researchers may need to check records in both NH DARM (Concord) and the New York State Archives (Albany) for the pre-independence period.

core_repositories.source_name: Vermont State Archives and Records Administration
core_repositories.description: Central state repository under the Secretary of State. Holds state government records, transferred county court records, vital records, military records, Vermont State Papers, governor's papers, legislative records, and naturalization records transferred from county courts. Essential first stop for Vermont genealogical research.

Premier genealogical research library in Vermont. The Leahy Library holds published genealogies, town histories, manuscript collections, family papers, church records, business records, and the Wilbur Collection of Vermontiana. Extensive vertical files organized by family name and town. Holdings include Italian mutual aid society records from Barre, French Canadian community documentation, and Green Mountain Boys-era materials.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Vermont Special Collections — Bailey/Howe Library
core_repositories.description: Major manuscript repository with extensive Vermont family papers, business records, and the Wilbur Collection. Holdings include Ethan Allen papers, early Vermont Republic documents, Lake Champlain commerce records, and Burlington city records. Finding aids available online.

State library with genealogy collection including Vermont town histories, county histories, published vital records, census microfilm, and newspaper microfilm. Vermont newspaper digitization project materials available here.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including town vital records, probate records, deed indexes, church records, cemetery transcriptions, and military records. Extensive microfilm holdings from partnerships with Vermont State Archives and town clerk offices.

familysearch_collections.source_name: FamilySearch — Vermont Vital Records (Town Clerk Records)
familysearch_collections.description: Microfilmed town clerk vital records covering births, marriages, and deaths from across Vermont. Many town volumes have been digitized and indexed. Coverage varies by town but many have records from the 1760s-1790s through the early 1900s.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Congress / Vermont State Library
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Vermont newspapers including the Vermont Journal (Windsor, from 1783), Burlington Free Press, Rutland Herald, Vermont Phoenix (Brattleboro), and smaller town papers. Coverage from the 1780s through mid-1900s.
Subscription database with additional digitized Vermont newspapers not in Chronicling America, including obituaries and social notices.

military_records.source_name: Vermont Adjutant General Reports — Revolutionary War through Civil War
military_records.description: Published reports listing Vermont soldiers by unit and service period. Includes Green Mountain Boys rosters, Revolutionary War muster rolls, War of 1812, and Civil War regimental histories (Vermont contributed heavily to the Union cause). Revised Roster of Vermont Soldiers (1892) covers the Civil War comprehensively.

Federal military service records, pension files, and bounty land warrants for Vermont soldiers from the Revolution through later conflicts. Includes Green Mountain Boys records and Battle of Bennington participants.

land_and_property.description: Vermont follows the New England pattern — land records (deeds, mortgages, liens) are recorded at the town level by town clerks, not at the county level. Each of Vermont's approximately 246 towns and cities maintains its own deed books. Some early volumes have been microfilmed by FamilySearch.

land_and_property.source_name: New Hampshire Grants and Vermont Republic Land Records
land_and_property.repository: Vermont State Archives, Middlesex / NH DARM, Concord
land_and_property.description: Original township charters granted by NH governors (1749-1764), New York counter-grants, and Vermont Republic land records. Published in Vermont State Papers. Essential for understanding original land ownership before town clerk records begin.

vital_records.source_name: Vermont Department of Health — Vital Records Office
vital_records.description: State-level vital records (birth, death, marriage, civil union, divorce) from 1857 to present. Older records at town clerk offices. Vermont was the first state to legalize civil unions (2000).

vital_records.source_name: Vermont Town Clerk Vital Records (pre-1857 and ongoing)
vital_records.description: Town clerks have maintained vital records (births, marriages, deaths) from earliest settlement, often predating state registration (1857) by decades. For records before 1857, the town clerk where the event occurred is the primary source. FamilySearch has microfilmed many town vital record volumes.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Diocese of Burlington Archives — Catholic Records
church_and_cemetery.description: Sacramental registers (baptism, marriage, burial) from Catholic parishes across Vermont. Critical for French Canadian families (northern counties) and Italian granite/marble worker families (Washington and Rutland counties). Diocese covers all of Vermont.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Congregational Library and Archives — Vermont Church Records
church_and_cemetery.description: Records of Vermont Congregational churches including membership rolls, baptisms, marriages, deaths, and session minutes. Congregationalism was the dominant denomination in early Vermont, and many town churches deposited records at the Boston library.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Vermont Old Cemetery Association / Vermont Historical Society
church_and_cemetery.description: Statewide cemetery transcription project. Published volumes of inscriptions from hundreds of Vermont cemeteries organized by town and county. Available at VHS Leahy Library and Vermont State Library. FindAGrave and BillionGraves complement these efforts.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Vermont Genealogy (journal of the Genealogical Society of Vermont)
research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing 'Vermont Genealogy' (formerly 'Branches and Twigs'). Maintains surname files, queries, and Vermont family research resources.
Comprehensive free research guide for Vermont genealogy including county-by-county record inventories, repository guides, and research strategies.

Specialized society for French Canadian genealogy in New England. Library in Manchester NH with extensive Quebec parish register transcriptions and Franco-American family files. Essential for tracing Vermont's French Canadian immigrant families.

historical_counties_extinct.source_name: Historical County Information — Cumberland and Gloucester Counties
historical_counties_extinct.description: Vermont's present 14 counties have been stable since Lamoille County was formed in 1835. Two historical counties are notable: Cumberland County (organized 1768 under New York, covered southeastern Vermont, replaced by Windham County 1781) and Gloucester County (organized 1770 under New York, covered northeastern Vermont, never fully operational). Records from Cumberland County may be at the New York State Archives in Albany. Additionally, the pre-1777 'New Hampshire Grants' period means some town charters and early records are in New Hampshire's archives.
historical_counties_extinct.notes: Pre-1777: Territory claimed by both New Hampshire and New York. Town charters granted by NH (the 'New Hampshire Grants') while New York organized Cumberland and Gloucester counties. After 1777: Vermont Republic organized its own counties. All 14 current counties established by 1835.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Soundex Index to Canadian Border Entries — St. Albans District
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Immigration records for persons entering the US through the St. Albans district covering the entire Vermont-Quebec border. Soundex-indexed cards (1895-1924) with name, age, nationality, and destination. One of the most important sources for tracing French Canadian immigration to New England. Available on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA New England Region, Waltham MA
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Federal court naturalization records for Vermont. Includes declarations of intention and petitions from US District Court. Critical for French Canadian, Italian, and other immigrant communities.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Vermont State Archives — County Court Naturalization Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from Vermont county courts transferred to the state archives. Declarations of intention and petitions. Coverage varies by county but especially strong for Washington (Barre Italian community) and Rutland (marble workers) counties.
